1:
וַיִּזְכֹּר אֱלֹהִים אֶת-נֹחַ (wayyizkōr ʾĕlōhîm ʾet-nōaḥ) — "but God remembered Noah." Zākar in the covenantal sense: not the recovery of forgotten information but the active, faithful engagement of God on behalf of the one he has committed himself to. The same verb marks every major covenant-rescue: God remembered Abraham and rescued Lot (19:29), God remembered Rachel and opened her womb (30:22), God remembered his covenant with Abraham/Isaac/Jacob and heard Israel's groaning (Exod 2:24; 6:5). "God remembered" is the turning-point formula in every crisis.

וַיַּעֲבֵר אֱלֹהִים רוּחַ עַל-הָאָרֶץ (wayyaʿăber ʾĕlōhîm rûaḥ ʿal-hāʾāreṣ) — "and God caused a wind to pass over the earth." Rûaḥ (wind/spirit) echoes 1:2 (the Spirit of God hovering over the face of the waters). The same creative-breath that hovered over the primordial waters at the beginning now passes over the flood-waters to begin re-creation. The flood narrative frames itself as a new creation: the wind-over-waters at the start of both events is deliberate literary architecture.
2: וַיִּסָּכְרוּ מַעְיְנֹת תְּהוֹם — "the fountains of the deep were stopped/closed." Sākar (Niphal, to be stopped, shut) — the reversal of 7:11 (nibqĕʿû, burst open). Both the tĕhôm (deep) and the heavenly windows are closed by divine action. The Hebrew passive (Niphal) keeps the agency implied rather than stated — the closing happens as the divine will reverses the opening. The rain ceasing (kālaʾ, to restrain, withhold) and the fountains closing happen simultaneously: the two sources of the flood are shut off together.
3: וַיָּשֻׁבוּ הַמַּיִם מֵעַל הָאָרֶץ הָלוֹךְ וָשׁוֹב — "the waters returned from upon the earth, going and returning." The infinitive-absolute construction hālôḵ wāšôb (going and turning back — a progressive, gradual movement) expresses the manner of the water's recession: not sudden but steady and continuous. The same construction is used in Exod 15:19 (Pharaoh's horses going into the sea) — the infinitive absolute + finite verb gives the sense of ongoing, repeated action.
4: The ark resting on הָרֵי אֲרָרַט (hārê ʾărāraṭ) — "the mountains of Ararat." Ararat corresponds to the ancient kingdom of Urartu in what is now eastern Turkey/Armenia, around Lake Van. "Mountains of Ararat" (plural) suggests a mountain range rather than a single peak; the specific mountain (modern Mount Ararat = Ağrı Dağı, 5,137 m) was not identified as the ark's resting place until the medieval period. The seventeenth day of the seventh month: if the flood began on the 17th of the 2nd month (7:11), exactly 5 months (150 days) have elapsed, precisely matching v. 3's "hundred and fifty days."
5: The precise calendar notation continues: "the first day of the tenth month, the tops of the mountains became visible." The Hebrew verb nīrĕʾû (they were seen/appeared) is Niphal of rāʾāh — the mountains appear as the creation order reappears. The word rāʾāh is the verb used in 1:4 (God saw the light was good) and 1:10 (God saw the dry land) — creation's visibility is being restored.
6: מִקֵּץ אַרְבָּעִים יוֹם — "at the end of forty days." The forty-day unit recurs from the flood's initiation (7:12, 17) — Noah waits a full forty days after the mountains appear before opening the window. The window (ḥallôn) that Noah opens is different from the window/hatch (ṣōhar, a light-opening in the roof, 6:16) that God specified; the text uses distinct vocabulary for the observation window opened at this point.
7: אֶת-הָעֹרֵב (ʾet-hāʿōrēb) — "the raven." Ravens are unclean birds (Lev 11:15; Deut 14:14) and are scavengers who would have been drawn to floating carcasses — a possible reason why the raven does not return: it finds food on the floating dead. יָצוֹא וָשׁוֹב (yāṣôʾ wāšôb) — infinitive absolute + finite verb: the raven went "going and returning" continuously until the earth dried — it does not provide clean information about the flood's state.
8: The dove is sent to gather information the raven did not provide. יוֹנָה (yônāh) — "dove" — a clean bird (Lev 1:14; 5:7; 12:6, 8), associated with peace, love (Song 1:15; 2:14), and mourning (Isa 38:14; 59:11; Nah 2:7). The dove's return with the olive leaf (v. 11) will become an enduring symbol of peace and the earth's restoration — a meaning absent from the text but generated by the narrative's outcome.
9: וְלֹא-מָצְאָה הַיּוֹנָה מָנוֹחַ לְכַף-רַגְלָהּ — "the dove found no resting place for the sole of her foot." Mānoaḥ (resting place, rest) — from nûaḥ (to rest), the root of Noah's name (5:29: "this one will comfort us... from our nûaḥ"). The dove seeking mānoaḥ (rest) echoes Noah's own name and the creation-rest of 2:1-3 — the dove's inability to find rest mirrors the earth's unreadiness to receive rest again.
10: וַיָּחֶל עוֹד שִׁבְעַת יָמִים אֲחֵרִים — "and he waited yet another seven days." The seven-day interval recalls the seven-day wait before the flood began (7:4, 10) — Noah's patient observational discipline mirrors the structured waiting of the flood's onset. The Hiphil of ḥûl (to wait, to hold out) here: Noah actively holds himself in readiness rather than passively sitting.
11: עֲלֵה-זַיִת טָרָף בְּפִיהָ — "a freshly plucked olive leaf in her beak." Ṭārap (torn off, freshly plucked — from the root meaning to tear fresh food) — the olive leaf is green and freshly obtained, not a floating dead fragment. Olive trees root in soil and their presence (still alive, still bearing leaves) indicates that the earth is surfacing and vegetation is growing again. ʿAleh-zayit (olive leaf/branch) will carry its symbolic weight of peace and restoration across cultures from this point, though the text itself is straightforwardly about ground-vegetation as the indicator of earth-recovery.
12: לֹא-יָסְפָה שׁוּב-אֵלָיו עוֹד — "it did not return to him again." The dove's non-return is the definitive signal: the earth is habitable for birds, which means it is habitable for land creatures. The narrative economy is precise — the three-stage sending of the raven (no result), dove-return-empty, dove-return-with-leaf, dove-no-return — gives the reader a progressive revelation of earth's recovery that matches Noah's own information situation.
13: וַיָּסַר נֹחַ אֶת-מִכְסֵה הַתֵּבָה — "Noah removed the covering of the ark." Miḵsāh (covering, roof covering) — the word is used for the tabernacle's covering (Exod 26:14; 35:11; 36:19). The removal of the covering makes the ark open to the sky above — an un-sealing after the sealing of 7:16. פְּנֵי הָאֲדָמָה — "the face/surface of the ground" — the same phrase used in 2:6 and throughout; the ground (ʾăḏāmāh) from which Adam was formed is now visible again, drying out.
14: The calendar precision completes: from 7:11 (17th day, 2nd month, year 600) to 8:14 (27th day, 2nd month, year 601) = exactly one year and ten days. The flood narrative operates with the precision of an annual calendar — 12 months (360 days by the 30-day month reckoning) plus the extra days. Some interpreters read a solar calendar of 364 days here. In any case, the narrative's calendrical specificity anchors the flood in datable time.
15: God's command to leave follows the same structure as God's command to enter (6:18-21; 7:1-3): divine speech initiating the major transition. Noah did not leave on his own initiative when he saw the ground was dry (v. 13) — he waited for the divine word. The obedience pattern of the entire flood narrative (Noah "did all that the LORD commanded him," 6:22; 7:5, 9, 16) reaches its final expression in waiting for the exit-command before exiting.
16: צֵא מִן-הַתֵּבָה (ṣēʾ min-hattēbāh) — "go out from the ark" — the command uses the same imperative structure as the entrance command of 7:1 (bōʾ, come/enter). The chiastic correspondence between entry and exit mirrors the structural chiasm of the flood narrative as a whole: creation → un-creation → re-creation.
17: הוֹצֵא אִתְּךָ כָּל-הַחַיָּה — "bring out with you all the living creatures." The Hiphil of yāṣāʾ (to cause to go out, bring out) — the same verb used for the Exodus (yāṣāʾ in Exod 13:3; 14:11; 20:2 and hundreds of other Exodus references). The repeated fruitfulness mandate — "be fruitful and teem on the earth, and be fruitful and multiply on the earth" — recycles the creation blessing of 1:22 (animals) and 1:28 (humanity), now re-issued to the post-flood world.
18: The exit is narrated with the same parallel structure as the entry (7:7, 13-16). The listing of Noah, his sons, his wife, and his sons' wives mirrors 7:7 and 7:13 — the family unit is complete and intact. The repetition validates that God's preservation promise (6:18) was fully realized: the entire household survived.
19: כֹּל הַחַיָּה כָּל-הָרֶמֶשׂ וְכָל-הָעוֹף כֹּל רוֹמֵשׂ עַל-הָאָרֶץ — the comprehensive listing of living creatures exiting the ark mirrors the creation sequence and the flood's comprehensive destruction. Lĕmišpĕḥōtêhem — "according to their families/clans" — a kinship-organizing term used in genealogical and census lists; the ark-exit is framed as the beginning of the new order of families and nations that ch. 10 will elaborate.
20: מִזְבֵּחַ (mizbēaḥ) — "altar" — Noah builds the first explicitly named altar in the biblical narrative (the altars of Cain and Abel, 4:3-4, are implied but not named). מִכֹּל הַבְּהֵמָה הַטְּהֹרָה וּמִכֹּל הָעוֹף הַטָּהוֹר — "from every clean animal and every clean bird" — the clean/unclean distinction justified by the seven pairs of clean animals (7:2-3) is here revealed: the surplus was for sacrifice. Noah's first act upon leaving the ark is worship — the building of the altar and the offering of burnt offerings (ʿōlōt) establishes the post-flood world on the basis of sacrifice and worship rather than personal achievement.
21:
וַיָּרַח יְהוָה אֶת-רֵיחַ הַנִּיחֹחַ (wayyāraḥ YHWH ʾet-rêaḥ hannîḥōaḥ) — "and the LORD smelled the pleasing aroma." The anthropomorphic language is frank: God smells and is pleased. The term rêaḥ hannîḥōaḥ (aroma of rest/satisfaction) is the technical phrase used for the pleasing sacrifice smell throughout Leviticus and Numbers (Lev 1:9, 13, 17; 2:2, 9; 3:5; Num 15:3-24, etc.) — the Noahic sacrifice uses Levitical sacrificial vocabulary, grounding Sinai's cultic system in the post-flood covenant.

יֵצֶר לֵב הָאָדָם רַע מִנְּעֻרָיו (yēṣer lēb hāʾādām raʿ minĕʿūrāyw) — "the inclination/impulse of the human heart is evil from his youth." Yēṣer (formation, inclination — from yāṣar, the potter's verb of 2:7) is the word used in 6:5 for the pre-flood judgment: "every yēṣer of the thoughts of his heart was only evil continually." The situation is unchanged: God promises never again to curse the ground because of humanity despite the same evil inclination that triggered the flood. The post-flood grace is explicitly given against the background of unchanged human nature — the covenant is not based on moral improvement but on divine commitment.
22: עֹד כָּל-יְמֵי הָאָרֶץ — "while all the days of the earth remain." The temporal phrase frames the covenant of seedtime/harvest, cold/heat, summer/winter, day/night as conditional on the earth's endurance — not eternal in an absolute sense, but as long as the present world-order lasts. The six pairs (seed/harvest, cold/heat, summer/winter, day/night — plus implicitly the day/night pair) form the natural ordering of life: God commits to maintain the cosmic regularity that makes agriculture, seasons, and human life possible. This is not the Noahic covenant of ch. 9 (which involves blood and the rainbow sign) but a natural-order commitment that underlies it.
